memoryArtifacts.description: Mayflower passenger list by William Bradford, 1650.

memoryArtifacts.headline: The Pilgrim Fathers Memorial, Southampton, Hampshire, England
memoryArtifacts.description: The Pilgrims actually began their voyage to the New World in Southampton, England, with two ships; the Mayflower and the Speedwell. As they sailed along England's southern shores, the Speedwell kept taking on water. They finally made a final stop at Plymouth, England and left the Speedwell there, while the Mayflower continued on.

memoryArtifacts.headline: Great Amwell Church
memoryArtifacts.description: Richard Warren and Elizabeth Walker were married April 14, 1610 at the Parish Church of St. John the Baptist in Great Amwell, Herfordshire, England.

popularStory.description:
The following is from The Great Migration Begins: Immigrants to New England, 1620-1633, Vol. III (P-W) by Robert Charles Anderson (NEHGS: Boston, 1995), pages 1935-1936:

Estate: In the 1623 Plymouth …

burialPlace.address: Burial Hill, Plymouth Colony, British Colonial America

person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
The findagrave picture is for William Brewster.
His parents are not known.  He is not Richard son of William Warren and Alice Webbe (nor is he the son of William’s father, Christopher).  That is a different Richard who may be found here https://www.familysearch.org/tree/person/details/GZX5-381 
—————————————-
“Richard Warren's English origins and ancestry have been the subject of much speculation, and countless different ancestries have been published for him, without a shred of evidence to support them.”http://mayflowerhistory.com/warren. From Silver Book Vol 18 page 1: "that he was from London is all we know about the origin of Richard Warren despite considerable research to learn more."

He received two acres in the "Division of Land" in 1623 and the family is listed in the "Division of Cattle" in 1627. The "Division of Cattle" document listed all of the people who lived in the colony - essentially creating a census of inhabitants. Settling in America also required a lot of money, which the Separatists did not have. For that, they formed a joint-stock company with merchant adventurers. In return for financial backing, the Separatists would send the investors their goods and profits from America for seven years, keeping only what they needed to survive. After seven years, the investors would release the colonists from service, dissolve the company, and distribute the assets among the colonists and investors.

Richard Warren very likely married Elizabeth Walker, at Great Amwell, Hertfordshire, on April 14, 1610. Elizabeth Walker was the daughter of Augustine Walker of Great Amwell. Warren came from London and was called a “merchant” of that city. Richard Warren was one of those very few English merchants who signed on to make the Mayflower voyage along with the Leiden contingent. At the time of the Mayflower’s voyage in 1620, Richard and his wife had five daughters: Mary, Ann, Sarah, Elizabeth and Abigail. But Richard came on the Mayflower alone.  On November 9/19, 1620, after about 3 months at sea, including a month of delays in England, they spotted land, which was the Cape Cod Hook, now called Provincetown Harbor. After several days of trying to get south to their planned destination of the Colony of Virginia, strong winter seas forced them to return to the harbor at Cape Cod hook, where they anchored on November 11. The Mayflower Compact was signed that day. He participated in some of the early explorations of Cape Cod, when a suitable settlement location was being searched for. One such extensive exploration began on Wednesday, December 6, 1620 in freezing weather using the ship’s shallop, a light, shallow-water boat with oars and sails which was navigated by two pilots, with a master gunner and three sailors. In 1623 Warren felt that conditions were right to bring his family over from England, and they arrived that year on the Anne. He was a major landholder in the early colony. Richard Warren died of unknown causes, possibly sometime in 1628, exact date unknown. Because all seven of Richard Warren and Elizabeth nee Walker's children survived and had families, they have very many descendants today.

zh.lifeSummary: Richard Warren於大約1585年出生在英國。他於1610年4月14日與Elizabeth Walker在英國賀特福爾格亞特 阿維爾結婚。他們至少是2個兒子和5個女兒的父母。他於1620年移民到布利提申 可隆尼亞爾 亞美利加普萊姆斯 可隆尼。他於1609年在英國倫敦被列為merchant, a draper in london。他去世於1628年在布利提申 可隆尼亞爾 亞美利加普萊姆斯 可隆尼享年44歲，埋葬在美國麻薩諸塞州普萊姆斯普萊姆斯布利亞爾 希爾。

nameMeanings.name: Warren
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from the Middle English (Old French) personal name Warin, Werin, a borrowing of ancient Germanic Warino, a short form of various compound names based on the element warin ‘protection, shelter’ or ‘guard’. Compare Waring .

English and Irish (of Norman origin): habitational name from La Varrenne in Seine-Maritime, France, named with a Gaulish element probably descriptive of alluvial land or sandy soil. This was the name of a major Norman family after the Conquest. In Ireland, this name has been Gaelicized as Bharain.
Irish: adopted as an English form of Gaelic Ó Murnáin (see Murnane , Warner ).

variants.name: Warrens
meanings: English: variant of Warren with genitival or excrescent -s. This surname is now rare in Britain.

variants.name: Warrener
meanings: English (of Norman origin): occupational name from Old French warrennier ‘warrener’, an officer employed to watch over game in a park or game preserve. Compare Warren .

variants.name: Murnane
meanings: Irish (Munster): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Murnáin, a shortened and dissimilated form of Ó Manannáin ‘descendant of Manannán’, name borne in Celtic mythology by a sea god associated with the Isle of Man. All three syllables appear in the variant Marrinan. This name has also been Anglicized as Warren and Warner .

variants.name: Warne
meanings: occasionally a habitational name from Wearne in Somerset. The place takes its name from a lost stream-name based on British verno- ‘alder, swamp’.

variants.name: Waring
meanings: English (Lancashire and Yorkshire): variant of Warren , from the Norman personal name Warin, derived from ancient Germanic Warino, a short form of various compound names based on the element warin ‘protection, shelter’ or ‘guard’. The name was popular in France and among the Normans (see Guerin ).

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: The First English Colony is Established in America
historicEvents.description: England established its first colony in North America in 1585 as a way to generate additional wealth. The colony was named Virginia, after the virgin Queen Elizabeth I.

historicEvents.title: Elizabeth I Signs Death Warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots
historicEvents.description: Elizabeth I signed the death warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots, on February 1, 1587, as she was found guilty of plotting to assasinate Elizabeth. Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England on February 8, 1587.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Elizabeth I.org - Mary Queen of Scots

historicEvents.title: The Honourable East India Company
historicEvents.description: The Honourable East India Company, a British joint-stock company, was established in 1600 for trade in the Indian Ocean region. At its height, the British East India Company had a private army which was twice the size of the British Army, ruled large sections of India, and revenues in the millions.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Historic UK - The East India Company

historicEvents.description: In 1620, after 2 rough months at sea, 102 Pilgrims landed at Plymouth Rock and established one of the very first English settlements in America.

historicEvents.title: The Pilgrims Board the Mayflower
historicEvents.description: "The Pilgrims boarded the Mayflower in search of religious freedom in the New World in September 1620. The Mayflower set sail from Plymouth, England, with 102 passengers, 40 of whom called themselves ""Saints."" After two stormy months at sea, the ship reached the New World. Nearly half of the group died during their first winter due to malnutrition and the harsh New England winter."

en.lifeSummary: Richard Warren was born about 1585, in England. He married Elizabeth Walker on 14 April 1610, in Great Amwell, Hertfordshire, England. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 5 daughters. He immigrated to Plymouth Colony, British Colonial America in 1620. In 1609, his occupation is listed as merchant, a draper in london in London, England. He died in 1628, in Plymouth Colony, British Colonial America, at the age of 44, and was buried in Burial Hill, Plymouth, Plymouth, Massachusetts, United States.

fr.lifeSummary: Richard Warren est né environ 1585, à Angleterre. Il a épousé Elizabeth Walker le 14 avril 1610, à Great Amwell, Hertfordshire, Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 5 filles. Il a immigré au Plymouth Colony, Amérique coloniale britannique en 1620. En 1609, sa profession de son indiquée est merchant, a draper in london à Londres, Angleterre. Il est décédé en 1628, à Plymouth Colony, Amérique coloniale britannique, à l’âge de 44 ans, et a été enterré à Burial Hill, Plymouth, Plymouth, Massachusetts, États-Unis.

ja.lifeSummary: Richard Warrenは約1585年にイギリスで生まれた。1610年4月14日にイギリスハートフォードシュアイアグレアト アムウエルでElizabeth Walkerと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも2人の息子と5人の娘の親であった。彼は1620年にイギリス植民地アメリカプリモウス コロニーに移住した。1609年，イギリスロンドンでの職業はmerchant, a draper in londonと記録されている。彼は1628年にイギリス植民地アメリカプリモウス コロニーで44歳で死去し，アメリカ合衆国マサチューセッツ州プリモウスプリモウスブリアル ヒルにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Richard Warren(은)는 대략 1585년 영국에서 태어났다. 그는 1610년 4월 14일 영국 허트퍼드실르 그리트 아웰에서 Elizabeth Walker(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 다섯 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1620년에 브리티시 콜론이앨 어머릭아 플림우스 콜론이로 이민을 떠났다. 1609년, 그의 직업은 영국 런던에서 merchant, a draper in london이었다. 그는 1628년 브리티시 콜론이앨 어머릭아 플림우스 콜론이에서 44세의 나이로 사망했으며 미국 매사추세츠 주 플림우스 플림우스 버이앨 힐에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Richard Warren nasceu aproximadamente 1585, em Inglaterra. Ele casou-se com Elizabeth Walker em 14 de abril de 1610, em Great Amwell, Hertfordshire, Inglaterra. Eles tiveram pelo menos 2 filhos e 5 filhas. Ele imigrou para o Plymouth Colony, British Colonial America em 1620. Em 1609, sua profissão aparece como merchant, a draper in london em London, Inglaterra. Ele faleceu em 1628, em Plymouth Colony, British Colonial America, com 44 anos, e foi sepultado em Burial Hill, Plymouth, Plymouth, Massachusetts, Estados Unidos da América.

ru.lifeSummary: Richard Warren родился около 1585 г. в местности Англия. Он женился на женщине по имени Elizabeth Walker 14 апреля 1610 г. в местности грит амвелл, хиртфордшир, Англия. У них было по крайней мере 2 сына и 5 дочерей. Он иммигрировал в плимоут колони, бритиш колоняль амирика в 1620 году. В 1609 году он работал по профессии merchant, a draper in london в местности Лондон, Англия. Он умер в 1628 г. в местности плимоут колони, бритиш колоняль амирика в возрасте 44 лет и был похоронен в местности буряль хилл, плимоут, плимоут, Массачусетс, Соединенные штаты.

es.lifeSummary: Richard Warren nació aproximadamente 1585, en Inglaterra. Se casó con Elizabeth Walker el 14 de abril de 1610, en Great Amwell, Hertfordshire, Inglaterra. Fueron padres de por lo menos 2 hijos y 5 hijas. Inmigró a Plymouth Colony, British Colonial America en 1620. En 1609, su ocupación registrada era merchant, a draper in london en London, Inglaterra. Murió en 1628, en Plymouth Colony, British Colonial America, a la edad de 44 años, y fue sepultado en Burial Hill, Plymouth, Plymouth, Masachusets, Estados Unidos.
